s: A tower drilled with 225 holes — each once held a severed head
w: Built by Alauddin Khalji in the 13th century, this squat tower bristles with 225 iron-ringed holes — one for every thief's head the Sultan had displayed as warning. Now it sits behind a residential gate off Aurobindo Marg, utterly forgotten. The Tower of Thieves.
_facts: 13th-century minaret with 225 holes used to display severed heads of thieves
Built during the reign of Alauddin Khalji of the Khalji dynasty
Located just off Aurobindo Marg in the Hauz Khas area, South Delhi
Name translates literally to 'Tower of Thieves'

s: A Tughlaq mosque like a fortress — roofed, windowed, bewildering
w: One of the strangest mosques in Delhi: almost entirely roofed over, 85 domes creating a labyrinthine interior lit by grilled windows — 'khirki' means 'window.' Built around 1380 by Feroz Shah Tughlaq's prime minister, it is partly ruined but village life still presses in at the edges. Disorienting at noon.
_facts: Built c. 1380 by Khan-i-Jahan Junan Shah, prime minister of Feroz Shah Tughlaq
One of the few surviving covered mosques in India, roofed by 85 domes
Name means 'window' — grilled windows are its distinctive feature
Located in Khirki Village, part of the medieval city of Jahanpanah

s: Tughlaq's great Friday mosque, swallowed by a village
w: Built around 1343 as the grand Friday mosque of Jahanpanah, Muhammad bin Tughlaq's fourth city, this 94 by 90 metre court stands largely intact inside dense Begumpur village, ringed by auto-repair shops and washing lines. Empire-scale ambition, neighbourhood-scale afterlife.
_facts: Built c. 1343 CE as the Jami Masjid of Jahanpanah, fourth medieval city of Delhi
Measures approximately 94 by 90 metres, one of the largest medieval mosques in India
Located in the urban village of Begumpur, surrounded by ordinary residential life
Attributed to Khan-i-Jahan Maqbul, prime minister of the Tughluq dynasty

s: Seven arches in a city wall that once held back floodwater
w: A seven-arched weir and city-gate built by Muhammad bin Tughlaq around 1325 to trap floodwater from a Yamuna tributary, forming Jahanpanah's southern rampart. Chambers above the sluices may once have served as garrison rooms. Still standing, still damp. The name means 'seven bridge.'
_facts: Ancient water-harvesting dam built during the reign of Sultan Muhammad Shah Tughlaq (1325-1351)
Integral to the compound wall of Jahanpanah, the fourth medieval city of Delhi
Seven arched spans: 'sapt' means seven, 'pul' means bridge
Functions simultaneously as flood control infrastructure and city fortification

s: The library staircase where Humayun slipped and never recovered
w: On 24 January 1556, Emperor Humayun descended these octagonal stone steps with books in his arms when the azan called — he knelt, slipped, struck his head, and died three days later. Sher Shah Suri built this library-pavilion; Humayun used it as his personal observatory. The steps are still there.
_facts: 16th-century octagonal library-pavilion built by Sher Shah Suri within Purana Qila
Emperor Humayun fell on its steps on 24 January 1556 and died of his injuries three days later
Designed in Indo-Islamic, Timurid and Persian architectural style
The only surviving palace structure within Purana Qila fort

s: A 1530s Mughal tomb in a traffic roundabout — nobody knows whose
w: Stranded on a Mathura Road island since the 1530s, this octagonal mausoleum predates Humayun's Tomb and contains what is believed to be the earliest painted Mughal ceiling in India — gold and lapis lazuli, still faintly visible. The builder is unknown. The occupant is unknown. The mystery stands.
_facts: Octagonal Mughal mausoleum from the 1530s, among the earliest Mughal buildings in India
Contains the earliest known painted Mughal ceiling, using pure gold and lapis lazuli
Neither the builder's nor the occupant's identity is known
Situated at a traffic intersection near the Nizamuddin complex on Mathura Road

s: A blue dome, an unknown grave, possibly older than Humayun's Tomb
w: Just outside the wall of Humayun's Tomb complex, the Blue Dome may predate its famous neighbour — historians disagree. Its glazed tile cladding is mostly gone. The person buried inside is completely unidentified: perhaps a servant of a Mughal noble, perhaps someone who died before Humayun was born. Nothing is settled.
_facts: Historical tomb with uncertain date; possibly predates Humayun's Tomb
Originally covered in glazed blue tiles, most of which have been lost
The occupant's identity is disputed — possibly buried during Jahangir's reign
Part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site of Humayun's Tomb complex

s: Where the ninth Sikh Guru was beheaded on Aurangzeb's orders
w: On 11 November 1675, Guru Tegh Bahadur was executed here on Aurangzeb's orders after refusing to convert to Islam. A small shrine was built in 1783 by Baghel Singh on the martyrdom site. Since 1979 the Sikh regiment of the Indian Army salutes this gurdwara before saluting the President — the only regiment to salute twice on Republic Day.
_facts: Marks the execution site of the ninth Sikh Guru, Guru Tegh Bahadur, on 11 November 1675
Built in 1783 by Baghel Singh; the Mughal Kotwali that previously stood here was demolished after 1857
Since 1979 the Sikh regiment of the Indian Army salutes this shrine before saluting the President on Republic Day

s: Delhi's oldest surviving temple — Mehrauli, still in use
w: Said by local priests to be the only surviving temple from Delhi's pre-Sultanate era still actively used. Dedicated to Yogmaya, a sister-form of Krishna, it was rebuilt by king Hemu in the 16th century after earlier destruction, and received an Islamic-style hall under Aurangzeb. Mehrauli residents still call the annual Phool Walon ki Sair fair 'her festival.'
_facts: Claimed to be the only surviving pre-Sultanate temple in Delhi still in active use
Dedicated to goddess Yogmaya, considered a sister of Krishna
Reconstructed by king Hemu (Samrat Vikramaditya Hemu) in the 16th century
A rectangular Islamic-style hall was added during Aurangzeb's reign

s: Legend says it was built from the proceeds of one grain of lentil
w: The story goes that Sikandar Lodi's prime minister found a single grain of moth dal on a tour of inspection, sold it, saved more, and accumulated over years a fortune sufficient to build this elegant 1505 mosque. Whether true or wishful accounting, it remains one of the finest examples of Lodi architecture.
_facts: Built in 1505 by Wazir Miya Bhoiya, Prime Minister during the reign of Sikander Lodi
Legend says construction was funded by savings accumulated grain by grain from a single moth lentil
Considered one of the finest examples of Lodi-dynasty mosque architecture
Located in South Delhi near South Extension

s: The Mad Sultan's Thousand Pillars — ruins in a South Delhi lane
w: This rubble mound and surviving watchtower is all that remains of the Hazar Sutun — 'Thousand Pillars' — the audience hall of Muhammad bin Tughlaq, the sultan Ibn Battuta visited and described in his travels. Now embedded in a residential street, crows on the ramparts.
_facts: Ruins of the Hazar Sutun (Thousand Pillars), Muhammad bin Tughlaq's royal audience hall in Jahanpanah
Visited and described by Moroccan traveller Ibn Battuta during his time at the Tughlaq court
Muhammad bin Tughlaq (r. 1325-1351) was known as 'The Eccentric Prince'
Surviving tower sits in the residential neighbourhood of Sarvapriya Vihar

s: A Sufi shrine where the last Mughal wished to be buried
w: Rulers built additions here for eight centuries: Iltutmish a stepwell, Sher Shah Suri a gateway, Bahadur Shah I a mosque. The last Mughal emperor, Bahadur Shah Zafar, asked to be buried beside this 13th-century Chishti saint. The British exiled him to Rangoon after 1857, where he died. His wish was never granted.
_facts: Dargah of Sufi saint Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki (1173-1235), disciple of Mu'in al-Din Chishti of Ajmer
Successive rulers built additions: Iltutmish (stepwell), Sher Shah Suri (gateway), Bahadur Shah I (Moti Masjid)
Last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar wished to be buried here but was exiled to Rangoon by the British after 1857
Adjacent to Zafar Mahal in Mehrauli village

s: A sulphur-smelling stepwell built for a Sufi saint, 13th century
w: Sultan Iltutmish built this stepwell near the dargah of his spiritual master Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki. 'Gandhak' means sulphur — the water reportedly carried a mineral smell, prized as medicinal. Hidden down Dargha Road in Mehrauli, it sits in shade below street level, green-tinged and mostly forgotten between the Qutb tourists and the dargah pilgrims.
_facts: Built by Sultan Iltutmish (r. 1211-1236) near the dargah of Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki
'Gandhak' means sulphur — the well was reportedly known for sulphurous-smelling water
Located on Dargha Road in Mehrauli, close to the Qutb complex
Iltutmish built it in honour of his spiritual guide

s: Built over Indraprastha — a Mahabharata city under the archaeologists
w: Humayun began this fort as Dinpanah; his Afghan rival Sher Shah Suri seized and finished it. Beneath both lies something older: excavations found Painted Grey Ware pottery dating to around 1000 BCE, lending weight to the tradition that this Yamuna ridge is Indraprastha, the Pandavas' capital. The question is officially still open.
_facts: Built jointly by Mughal Emperor Humayun and Afghan Sultan Sher Shah Suri in the 1530s-40s
Archaeological excavations found Painted Grey Ware pottery (c. 1000 BCE), supporting the Indraprastha identification
Humayun called it Dinpanah ('refuge of faith'); Sher Shah renamed it Shergarh
Located on the Yamuna bank near Pragati Maidan in New Delhi

s: A 4th-century iron column that has barely rusted in 1,600 years
w: Chandragupta II had this 7.2-metre column erected around the 4th or 5th century CE; it arrived at the Qutb complex later. The pillar has not significantly rusted in over 1,600 years — metallurgists now credit an unusually high phosphorus content forming a protective misawite layer. The Sanskrit inscription is still legible.
_facts: A 7.21-metre tall iron column constructed by Chandragupta II, probably 4th-5th century CE
Has resisted corrosion for over 1,600 years; attributed to high phosphorus content forming a protective iron hydrogen phosphate layer
Originally erected at a Vishnu temple, later moved to the Qutb complex at Mehrauli
Sanskrit inscription records Chandragupta's military victories

s: Glass Dome: glazed Lodi tiles, unknown dead, inscription says 1494
w: Built between 1489 and 1517, the 'glass dome' was once covered entirely in glazed blue tiles. The graves inside are unidentified — possibly Bahlul Lodi himself, possibly an unknown court family. An inscription in the adjoining mosque suggests 1494. Joggers pass it every morning. The names were never recorded.
_facts: Tomb from the Lodi dynasty, constructed between 1489 and 1517 CE; one mosque inscription suggests 1494
Name means 'glass dome' — originally covered in glazed blue tiles, now largely gone
Occupants are not definitively identified; possibly Bahlul Lodi or members of his court
Located in Lodi Gardens, a 90-acre park containing multiple Lodi and Sayyid dynasty tombs

s: The princess who helped dethrone her father is buried here
w: Roshanara Begum, Shah Jahan's second daughter, sided with her brother Aurangzeb when he deposed and imprisoned their father in 1658. She was rewarded with this garden estate; she is buried in the pavilion she built as her own tomb. The garden is a public park now. The princess sleeps under joggers and kite flyers.
_facts: Built by Roshanara Begum, second daughter of Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan
Roshanara supported Aurangzeb in the 1658 War of Succession that deposed Shah Jahan
She is buried in the garden in a pavilion she designed as her own mausoleum
Now a public park in Shakti Nagar, near the North Campus of Delhi University

s: India's largest crafts museum — and the murals it destroyed in renovation
w: One of India's largest crafts museums: tribal textiles, bronzes, terracottas, and a full-scale village complex of craftspeople's houses. In 2015, renovation contractors destroyed a room of murals by Madhubani master Ganga Devi, one of the collection's most prized pieces. National outcry; the murals survive only in photographs.
_facts: One of the largest crafts museums in India, run by the Ministry of Textiles
Holds tribal crafts, textiles, bronzes and a full-scale village complex from across India
In 2015, renovation work destroyed murals painted by celebrated Madhubani artist Ganga Devi
Located on the corner of Pragati Maidan, facing the Purana Qila complex
